perturbation.original_text: All such information in tangible form shall be returned to Disclosing Party promptly upon written request by Disclosing Party or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, whichever occurs first, and shall not thereafter be retained in any form by Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: All such information in tangible form shall be returned to Disclosing Party within ninety (90) days upon written request by Disclosing Party or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, whichever occurs first, and may thereafter be retained in archival form by Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The original requires prompt return of information with no retention. The modified version allows for retention in 'archival form,' contradicting the initial 'shall not thereafter be retained in any form'. This creates uncertainty about data handling practices after termination, especially if another section discusses destruction of all confidential information after termination (Section 4)
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement (including without limitation information in computer software or held in electronic storage media) shall be and remain in the property of Disclosing Party. All such information in tangible form shall be returned to Disclosing Party within ninety (90) days upon written request by Disclosing Party or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, whichever occurs first, and may thereafter be retained in archival form by Recipient. In lieu of return, such infor mation may be destroyed by the Recipient provided any such destruction shall be certified in writing to the Disclosing Party by one of Recipient’s duly authorized officers. No intellectual property rights, including but not limited to, licenses or rights under any patent, copyright, trademark or trade secret, are granted or are to be implied by this Agreement. Neither Party is obligated under this Agreement to purchase from or provide to the other Party any service or product or enter into any agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates prompt return of tangible information with no retention. The changed text allows retention in 'archival form.' This directly contradicts the original requirement and the implied expectation that the information should not be retained, creating an in-text contradiction. This is because the change introduces an exception to the rule of returning all information, which the original did not allow. Furthermore, the change also appears within the contradicted text, further highlighting the inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall maintain in confidence and shall not disclose to any person not a party hereto, unless permitted to do so under Section 2, or use or exploit in any way, without the Disclosing Party’s written agreement, any Confidential Information for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of such information, unless such information ceases to be Confidential Information prior to the end of such five -year period through no fault of Recipient, or Recipient an d Disclosing Party enter into a written agreement authorizing same.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall maintain in confidence and shall not disclose to any person not a party hereto, unless permitted to do so under Section 2, or use or exploit in any way, without the Disclosing Party’s written agreement, any Confidential Information for a period of two (2) years from the date of disclosure of such information for marketing purposes, and five (5) years for all other purposes , unless such information ceases to be Confidential Information prior to the end of such five -year period through no fault of Recipient, or Recipient an d Disclosing Party enter into a written agreement authorizing same.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a dual standard for the confidentiality period depending on the purpose, creating ambiguity. 'Marketing purposes' have a 2-year confidentiality period, while all other purposes have a 5-year period, unless such information ceases to be Confidential Information prior to the end of such five-year period through no fault of Recipient. It creates a contradiction with Section 1, where the Confidential Information is defined without differentiating its use, so the time to keep confident the data is 5 years, but this added phrase makes the contract ambigous.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information. a) “Confidential Information” means all information both tangible and intangible of a Disclosing Party which relates, respectively, to the above identified su bject matter, including, but not limited to, trade secrets, business and technical information and data, disclosed orally, visually, in writing, electronic media or by any other means, and that is marked in accordance with this Section 1. b) With respect to any tangible information that a Disclosing Party would like to be treated as Confidential Information under this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall mark such information as “Confidential” prior to disclosing it to the Recipient. c) With respect to any oral or visual communication or other intangible information which a Disclosing Party would like to be treated as Confidential Information under this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall notify Recipient of such fact at the time of disclosure and within fiftee n (15) days thereafter, Disclosing Party shall send Recipient a written memorandum outlining the information deemed to be Confidential Information. Such memorandum shall be marked “Confidential.”
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a dual standard for the confidentiality period (2 years for marketing, 5 years for other purposes). This contradicts the original definition of 'Confidential Information' in the 'Contradicted Text' section, which does not differentiate based on usage and implies a uniform 5-year confidentiality period. The change creates ambiguity and an in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type, making it unclear which confidentiality period applies in specific situations.

perturbation.original_text: Disclosing Party makes no representation or warranty to Recipient as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information provided by it and shall not have any liability or responsibility for errors or omissions in any Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Disclosing Party makes no representation or warranty to Recipient as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information provided by it, except for information explicitly marked as 'Verified Confidential' and shall not have any liability or responsibility for errors or omissions in any Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an exception to the 'no warranty' clause for 'Verified Confidential' information. This creates conflict with the initial part of the sentence that denies any warranty for completeness or accuracy in any Confidential information. This contradict in particular with section 1, where all the required criteria to make a data Confidential doesn't include it to be verified.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information. a) “Confidential Information” means all information both tangible and intangible of a Disclosing Party which relates, respectively, to the above identified su bject matter, including, but not limited to, trade secrets, business and technical information and data, disclosed orally, visually, in writing, electronic media or by any other means, and that is marked in accordance with this Section 1. b) With respect to any tangible information that a Disclosing Party would like to be treated as Confidential Information under this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall mark such information as “Confidential” prior to disclosing it to the Recipient. c) With respect to any oral or visual communication or other intangible information which a Disclosing Party would like to be treated as Confidential Information under this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall notify Recipient of such fact at the time of disclosure and within fiftee n (15) days thereafter, Disclosing Party shall send Recipient a written memorandum outlining the information deemed to be Confidential Information. Such memorandum shall be marked “Confidential.”
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the 'no warranty' clause for 'Verified Confidential' information. This directly contradicts the original text which stated no warranty applied to ANY Confidential Information, regardless of verification status. This creates a direct conflict because Section 1 defines 'Confidential Information' based on marking as 'Confidential,' not 'Verified Confidential.' Thus, the changed text creates a new, undefined category with different warranty terms, creating a contradiction with the existing definition and warranty disclaimers.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any actions under it shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania without regard to its conflicts of laws provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any actions under it shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, except for disputes related to intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of Delaware without regard to its conflicts of laws provisions.
perturbation.explanation: By introducing an exception for IP disputes under Delaware law, the agreement now has conflicting governing laws. Section 4 refers to intellectual property, so this creates an in-text contradiction when discussing remedies and ownership. This is contradicting the general statement of Pennsylvania Laws.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement (including without limitation information in computer software or held in electronic storage media) shall be and remain in the property of Disclosing Party. All such information in tangible form shall be returned to Disclosing Party within ninety (90) days upon written request by Disclosing Party or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, whichever occurs first, and may thereafter be retained in archival form by Recipient. In lieu of return, such infor mation may be destroyed by the Recipient provided any such destruction shall be certified in writing to the Disclosing Party by one of Recipient’s duly authorized officers. No intellectual property rights, including but not limited to, licenses or rights under any patent, copyright, trademark or trade secret, are granted or are to be implied by this Agreement. Neither Party is obligated under this Agreement to purchase from or provide to the other Party any service or product or enter into any agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the governing law, stating that Delaware law applies to IP disputes, while the original agreement specified Pennsylvania law for all actions. The contradicted text concerns ownership and intellectual property rights, directly relating to IP. This creates a clear conflict, as the agreement now inconsistently applies different laws to the same subject matter, making it uncertain which jurisdiction's laws govern the interpretation and enforcement of the intellectual property provisions in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement will be one (1) year from the Effective Date, unless it is terminated earlier by either party providing the other party with at least thirty (30) days prior written notice, or unless it is extended as mutually agreed upon by the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement will be one (1) year from the Effective Date, unless it is terminated earlier by either party providing the other party with at least thirty (30) days prior written notice, except in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, where termination is effective immediately, or unless it is extended as mutually agreed upon by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds an immediate termination clause for 'gross negligence or willful misconduct', contradicting the original 30-day notice requirement. This affects all sections relying on the agreement's term (like Section 5 regarding confidentiality duration), causing confusion over contractual obligations and creating an internal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall maintain in confidence and shall not disclose to any person not a party hereto, unless permitted to do so under Section 2, or use or exploit in any way, without the Disclosing Party’s written agreement, any Confidential Information for a period of two (2) years from the date of disclosure of such information for marketing purposes, and five (5) years for all other purposes , unless such information ceases to be Confidential Information prior to the end of such five -year period through no fault of Recipient, or Recipient an d Disclosing Party enter into a written agreement authorizing same.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an immediate termination clause for gross negligence or willful misconduct, overriding the original requirement of 30 days' prior written notice. This directly contradicts the contradicted text (Section 5), which specifies a fixed duration (two to five years) for maintaining confidentiality. The change introduces uncertainty regarding the agreement's duration, making it unclear whether the confidentiality obligations remain in effect if the agreement is terminated immediately due to gross negligence.
